title: TBM 413: In That Space Is Our Power
questions: Why do workplace change conversations feel like I have to prove my feelings and observations are real?
How do I respond when people say “that doesn’t happen in the real world” or “A-players don’t need that”?
What is my real goal in advocating for change—validation, acknowledgment, or a specific behavioral shift?
How can I use the space between stimulus and response to avoid escalation when I feel dismissed?
search_terms: being dismissed at work how to cope
need to be heard validation psychology
summary: This post explores why workplace change advocacy can feel profoundly unfair, especially when advocates are treated as if they must prove their perceptions while their need to be heard is dismissed. It offers reframes for handling identity threat, vulnerability, and motive attribution, and encourages advocates to clarify what they truly want before escalating. The guidance is aimed at people trying to influence how work gets done without losing their energy or agency.

title: TBM 272: Three Perspectives
questions: How can strengths or good intentions be hidden inside harsh or dismissive narratives from different roles?
How could bold action, stability, and balancing competing pressures work together instead of clashing?
In what ways might each group be seeing something real that the others are missing?
How can I act as a bridge between people with very different vantage points?
summary: The post presents three fictionalized but recognizable workplace narratives (CEO/founder, platform product manager, and VP) to illustrate how different roles experience the same company reality in conflicting ways. It’s aimed at leaders and practitioners who want a practical lens for reducing role-based misunderstandings, especially amid layoffs, burnout, and AI-driven urgency messaging. The core value is perspective-taking: treating each viewpoint as shaped by constraints and information, then looking for overlap and bridging actions.

title: TBM 343: "We Kind of Suck at That Right Now"
questions: How can I give honest feedback without sounding like I'm blaming individuals?
Why do people perceive my improvement-focused comments as offensive or threatening?
What communication practices help change agents avoid triggering defensiveness?
How do I balance optimism about systems with sensitivity to others' attribution style?
search_terms: how to give feedback without blame
how to apologize for offensive comments
summary: The post explores how a well-intended comment about team performance can land as personal insult or blame, particularly when delivered in front of a boss. It’s aimed at people who see themselves as helpers, advocates, or continuous-improvement champions and want practical guidance on communicating in ways that reduce threat and increase collaboration. The core emphasis is shifting from individual-focused attribution to collective opportunity framing while letting go of being right and sharing everything.

title: TBM 327: Hot Takes and Parochial Altruism
questions: How do I identify with an in-group at work, and how does that shape how I judge people outside the group?
What triggers me to slip into an us-vs-them mindset or simplistic labels, and what need is that serving?
How do job insecurity, FOMO, and shifting industry trends push me toward harsh judgment instead of curiosity?
When I encounter scapegoating rhetoric, how should I decide whether to engage and how to do so constructively?
search_terms: how to respond to divisive rhetoric
summary: The post argues that some tech “hot takes” function as parochial altruism by pairing in-group loyalty with out-group stereotyping and scapegoating. It connects this communication pattern to identity threat and broader industry contradictions like layoffs, scrutiny, and role insecurity. It’s aimed at professionals who want practical self-awareness and better ways to respond when divisive rhetoric shows up in their work communities.

title: The tough reality of being a "glue person"...
questions: What are the hidden downsides of being the “glue person” at work?
Why do glue people’s wins go unnoticed while their mistakes become public?
How can I manage workplace politics when I see more than others?
What practical steps help a glue person avoid burnout and career stagnation?
search_terms: how to handle being the glue person
career change strategy for people who feel stuck
summary: The post addresses the emotional and political realities of being the “glue person” who holds teams together, highlighting how their wins are often invisible while missteps are highly visible. It’s aimed at managers, cross-functional contributors, and career-minded professionals who feel drained by high visibility, flexibility demands, and resentment or competition around their advocacy. It offers practical self-protection tactics such as calling out wins, setting boundaries, compartmentalizing, and running small experiments to counter common failure modes.

title: TBM 315: The Self-Sealing Argument Trap
questions: How can I tell when I’m in a self-sealing argument trap during workplace feedback?
What’s the difference between an intentional bad actor and someone operating on autopilot within their belief system?
Which response tactics help me avoid escalating or surrendering in no-win debates?
How do popular management narratives (e.g., high agency, rapid adaptation) function as self-sealing rhetoric?
search_terms: name it to tame it conflict
summary: The post explains how “self-sealing arguments” operate in workplace conversations—claims structured so that any evidence or response only reinforces the original accusation. It’s for managers, team members, and anyone navigating feedback and conflict who wants practical ways to recognize the trap early and choose less self-defeating responses.

title: TBM 308: No Unforced Errors
questions: How can I avoid unforced errors when advocating for change in environments that punish mistakes?
What biases cause others to interpret my off days more harshly than they interpret others’ off days?
Where should I steer away from conversations with senior leaders because the upside is low and the hornet’s nest risk is high?
How can I manage my energy for the long term when I can’t always have a good day?
search_terms: lose the battle to win the war
summary: This post explores why advocates and change agents get judged harshly for off days and how common cognitive biases amplify reputational risk. It offers practical guidance for leaders—especially enablement and cross-hierarchy roles—on avoiding unforced errors, steering high-risk conversations, and conserving energy for long-term impact. It’s aimed at people doing thorny organizational change who want to stay effective without burning out.

title: TBM 302: Why Reflexive Thinkers Need to Take More Breaks
questions: Why do reflexive, open-minded people feel more drained at work than others?
How does navigating others’ binary certainty increase cognitive and emotional load?
What self-care practices help reflexive thinkers recover without losing nuance?
How can someone stay open-minded and bridge-building while avoiding burnout?
summary: The post argues that reflexive, cognitively flexible people experience higher mental and emotional costs at work because they must continuously process conflicting perspectives, manage cognitive dissonance, and respond to others’ binary certainty. It uses a meritocracy-belief contrast to illustrate how this extra “carrying” of paradoxes makes recovery harder. The message is aimed at knowledge workers who identify with the reflexive side and want strategies for staying open-minded without burning out.

title: TBM 299: Underperformer
questions: Should I describe someone as an underperformer or focus on their performance relative to expectations in context?
How do I avoid putting people (or myself) in a limiting box during feedback and evaluation?
What does it mean for managers to take responsibility for the environment when performance is weak?
Why do dysfunctional workplaces reduce the number of people raising concerns over time?
summary: This post argues that how managers frame performance—labeling the person versus evaluating performance in context—changes the beliefs and behaviors that follow. It is aimed at leaders and managers who want to give feedback and make decisions without ignoring environmental constraints or putting people into limiting boxes. It also connects these framing choices to how dysfunctional organizations select for certain coping behaviors over time.

title: TBM 271: The Biggest Untapped Opportunity
questions: How can we identify skilled pragmatists who are underutilized but not visible?
Why do standard engagement playbooks fail with employees who prefer working within the system?
What incentives and management practices cause talent to become bored or invisible?
How should leaders challenge skilled pragmatists without triggering politics or conflict?
search_terms: why employees are bored at work
summary: This post argues that many companies waste capacity by overlooking “skilled pragmatists”—reliable, system-oriented employees who are under-challenged and therefore less visible than low performers or political careerists. It is aimed at leaders and managers who want to improve productivity and reduce waste by diagnosing the real source of dysfunction and redesigning how work is challenged and recognized.

title: TBM 254: Self-Gaslighting and the Doubt Loop
questions: When was the last time I felt genuine accomplishment or pride at work, and what triggered it?
What patterns in my workplace cause me to doubt myself, and what do those situations have in common?
How am I minimizing or invalidating my own feelings and experiences at work?
What boundaries could protect me from absorbing low-level dysfunction and help me meet my needs?
search_terms: joy and pride as a signal of fulfillment
summary: This post explores how non-obvious workplace dysfunction can trigger a self-reinforcing doubt loop that leads people to self-gaslight and blame themselves for systemic issues. It’s aimed at professionals who feel eroded by chronic pressure, invalidation, or “success narrative” advice and want a way to re-anchor on what feels genuinely fulfilling. The piece also encourages boundary-setting and reflection on patterns, fit, and who benefits from the culture of doubt.

title: TBM 224: The Black Box
questions: Why does increasing visibility into team problems sometimes make things worse?
How can status reporting fail to explain what’s really driving quality and burnout?
What are the unintended feedback loops created by reactive quality firefighting?
Why do new managers with low context often choose planning/estimation fixes instead of root-cause changes?
search_terms: burnout caused by process feedback loops
cognitive load from exposing messy work
new manager low context problem solving
summary: This post uses a “black box” metaphor to explain how team burnout and low-quality work can be symptoms of hidden upstream dynamics rather than isolated problems. It’s aimed at leaders and managers who try to fix issues by demanding visibility, status checks, or “shining a light,” and shows how those interventions can create new inputs—stress, overwhelm, and resentment—leading to superficial planning-based solutions instead of root-cause improvement.

title: TBM 223: Why Aren't They Saying Anything?
questions: Why aren’t employees speaking up even when something seems broken?
How can leaders design feedback channels so people know how and where to raise issues, including those involving managers?
What prevents people from connecting symptoms like cross-team communication problems to root causes in org design?
Is relying on surveys or telling people to speak to their managers enough to surface real problems?
search_terms: how to encourage speaking up at work
how to raise issues with your manager
summary: The post explores why leaders often hear little about problems even when many employees notice them, arguing that silence is driven by multiple behavioral and organizational barriers rather than just a lack of psychological safety. It’s aimed at leaders and managers who rely on surveys or “tell your manager” approaches and want practical ways to remove specific obstacles to employee voice, especially when issues involve managers or root-cause org design problems.

title: TBM 212: A Problem vs. The Problem
questions: When someone asks for a clear definition of the problem, which level of causality are they actually asking for?
How do we distinguish an adverse effect from contributing factors and root causes in everyday work?
Why do problem discussions become political or identity-driven instead of solution-oriented?
What practices help teams communicate about problems more precisely?
summary: The post argues that teams often confuse different levels of causality when discussing “problems,” leading to circular conversations. It provides a practical framing of problems as adverse effects, contributing/immediate causes, and underlying/systemic factors, and explains how problem definitions become political negotiations. It’s aimed at practitioners in RCA, safety, incident analysis, and human factors who want clearer, more actionable problem communication.

title: TBM 208: "Do You Have Specific Examples?"
questions: How do I respond when someone asks me for specific examples during performance feedback or a systemic discussion?
What should leaders do to request more information without creating blame or singling out individuals?
How can I share systemic concerns without my examples being dismissed as edge cases?
How do I balance specific details with the broader pattern we’re trying to address?
search_terms: how to give examples in a meeting
summary: This post offers communication strategies for performance feedback and systemic leadership conversations when “specific examples” are requested. It’s aimed at managers and employees who want to reduce threat, blame, and power-dynamic anxiety while still balancing concrete details with the broader pattern of the problem.

title: TBM 6/52: Probably, Just, Maybe, and Perhaps
questions: What is my default explanation when things go wrong, and what action do I automatically take next?
How can I practice generating alternative explanations instead of stopping at my first attribution?
What next step can help us test uncomfortable but plausible explanations?
How aware are my teammates of their own belief systems and defaults?
summary: The post offers a simple leadership and problem-solving framework for breaking the automatic cycle of default explanations and default actions. It’s aimed at leaders and team members who want to improve self/other awareness, practice generating alternative explanations, and choose exploratory next steps when situations are complex or uncomfortable.

title: TBM 52/52: Perception Asymmetries (and Your Career)
questions: Why do my skills and experience not translate into the opportunities I want?
How can I learn what external people perceive about my work versus what my champions believe?
What causes different narratives about me to form in different social circles?
How do I solicit honest, potentially hurtful feedback without getting defensive?
search_terms: getting put in a box at work
summary: The post argues that career stagnation often comes from perception asymmetries—differences between what your champions see and what external stakeholders can observe. It explains how visible signals and social information sharing can create competing, self-reinforcing narratives, especially when your skills are hard to categorize. It is aimed at professionals seeking practical ways to calibrate external perceptions by asking for candid feedback.

title: TBM 49/52: Pyramid of Leadership Self/Other Awareness
questions: What are my default explanations when things go wrong at work?
Do I assume my defaults are universal or universally right?
How can I adapt my leadership approach to match others’ motivations and priorities?
When should I shift from individual blame to system-level causality?
search_terms: system causes vs individual blame Deming
product leadership collaboration with diverse teams
summary: This post offers a framework for product leaders to work more effectively with diverse individuals by examining the “default explanations” they reach for when problems arise. It distinguishes self-awareness from the conviction that one’s defaults are universal and optimal, and argues that better collaboration comes from curiosity and actively challenging one’s own causal narratives. It is aimed at leaders who want practical ways to reduce bias, avoid mono-culture assumptions, and calibrate leadership to others’ motivations.

title: TBM 43/52: Working With Very Confident People (Who Dismiss You)
questions: How do I stay effective when very confident people dismiss my views?
Why do I feel threatened, annoyed, or jealous in these interactions?
What should I do differently to avoid attribution bias and stereotyping at work?
How can I pick battles strategically when I can’t change someone’s beliefs directly?
search_terms: how to pick your battles at work
career strategy when people dismiss you
summary: This post is for people in tech and other workplaces who regularly encounter very confident colleagues or managers who dismiss their perspectives. It explores the internal threat response that such interactions trigger and offers practical mental models—like attribution bias and stereotype avoidance—to help readers protect their energy. The guidance emphasizes choosing strategic battles aligned to specific needs rather than trying to change someone’s beliefs directly.

title: TBM 22/52: The Perfect Manager/Leader
questions: Why is dysfunction or BS treated as the norm for managers and leaders?
Should managers be expected to shield direct reports from problems above them?
How can organizations encourage sanity and self-care for leaders and teams?
What would a healthy, fun workplace look like in practice?
summary: This post critiques the pressure on tech managers to be “perfect” leaders who absorb dysfunction, manage up, and suppress emotions. It argues that healthy, joyful workplaces are achievable and that leaders should not be expected to carry the full weight of organizational BS. The piece is for managers and organizational leaders looking to reduce stress and promote sanity and self-care at work.

title: TBM 20/54: "You're Making This Too Complicated"
questions: How do I tell the difference between analysis paralysis and productive deep work?
Why do people interpret my approach as “too complicated” or “not bringing solutions”?
What should a team do when members have different action vs analysis defaults?
How can I adjust my style to be more empathetic and reduce triggers?
search_terms: how to handle “you’re making this too complicated”
summary: The post uses home organization styles as a metaphor for how people approach work—ranging from deep exploration to action-first execution. It’s aimed at team members and leaders who struggle with misreads like “analysis paralysis,” “not bringing solutions,” or “you’re making this too complicated.” The core guidance is to build self-awareness, discuss different approaches explicitly, and assume good intent to reduce trigger-driven misunderstandings.

title: TBM 3/52: That’s Not What They Are Hearing
questions: Why do my conversations with my manager or team member keep going in circles?
What am I (or they) really feeling underneath the discussion about the problem?
How do my beliefs about resilience and self-focus versus community affect how I interpret feedback?
What should change if the current communication style isn’t working?
search_terms: surface vs underlying feelings at work
how to talk to a manager when you feel stuck
not sure how to be good at my job
summary: The post explores why manager-employee conversations can become circular when each person responds to the surface issue while missing the deeper emotional need underneath. It’s aimed at managers and team members who feel misunderstood, stuck, or unsafe enough to express what they’re really feeling, and it argues that changing the felt sense—not just the wording—can open a path forward.

title: TMB 42/52: The Integrator Burden
questions: How can organizations recognize and reward cross-team integration work that is currently invisible?
What is the “wolf behind your tree” that draws you into the messy middle of the org?
Why do formal structures and incentives fail to support the emotional labor of connecting teams?
How can leaders elevate the profile of underrepresented groups who carry integration burdens?
search_terms: double standards for product managers testers analysts
summary: This post explores the “integrator burden”: the emotional labor of connecting, translating, and cohering across teams when formal structures and incentives fail to recognize that work. It’s aimed at managers and cross-functional contributors—especially underrepresented people—who feel pulled into the messy middle and may be burning out from unacknowledged responsibility. The piece argues for self-care and for organizational changes to elevate and reward integration work.

title: TBM 14/52: Discussing Complex Problems with Leaders
questions: How do I bring up complex systemic issues to leaders without triggering defensiveness?
What should I say when leaders demand specifics like who did what and what should be done?
Is it better to speak for others or to speak about my own needs and response?
What is a good next step when the problem is thorny and has many moving parts?
search_terms: how to talk to leaders about complex problems
facilitation ask next step without solution
summary: This post offers communication guidance for systems thinkers and empaths who need to raise complex, ambiguous problems with leaders who want actionable specifics. It argues that speaking for others can trigger threat responses, and that more effective conversations clarify your own needs, define an “ask” for the leader, and use safe-to-fail next steps to reduce complexity.

title: TBM 37/53: Talk to Your Manager, Take the Survey
questions: Why does feedback from engagement surveys and managers fail to reach the right level of the organization?
When should organizations use surveys versus retrospectives for organizational learning?
How can we reduce bias and filtering when feedback needs to travel across functions?
What feedback mechanisms work best for remote teams to avoid siloed communication?
search_terms: why employee feedback doesn't lead to action
summary: This post argues that when feedback needs to address non-local, cross-functional issues, relying on manager-to-top escalation and engagement surveys often fails due to bias, representativeness problems, and lack of trust in follow-through. It is aimed at leaders and product/software teams looking for better organizational learning mechanisms—especially large-scale retrospectives and continuous improvement events—to counter siloed communication in remote work.

title: TBM 23/53: Privilege & Credibility
questions: How should I pitch a new initiative to be inclusive across different identities and experiences?
Is “starting together” truly equitable, or does it privilege certain communication styles?
When people are solution-forward to gain credibility, what does that reveal about bias and power?
How can I adjust my feedback and facilitation to reduce credibility and privilege gaps?
search_terms: credibility bias women of color tech
summary: The post reflects on a conversation about how credibility is distributed unevenly in tech workplaces, particularly for women of color. It challenges the author’s assumption that starting together with problem exploration is automatically inclusive, arguing that some people are pressured to pitch solutions to be taken seriously. It’s aimed at leaders and collaborators who want to improve how they frame initiatives and feedback to address privilege and bias.
